metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'looks like an email' predicate used in §3's `/user/search` fallback (e.g., `'@' in assignee and '.' in assignee.split('@')[-1]` or reference to a stdlib utility) so the branch condition is unambiguous.
findings.finding: Section 3's fallback decision rule 'falling back to `assignee` itself when it already looks like an email' never defines what 'looks like an email' means — no regex, no stdlib reference, no character predicate is specified.
findings.location: Section 3 (§3 — `/user/search` bootstrap, last sentence of first paragraph)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document that `outbound_differ.py`'s new `identity.jira_account_id` call is wrapped in a try/except (or lazy-imported with a None-fallback guard) so standalone-loaded test contexts do not raise ImportError when the rebar package is absent from sys.path.
findings.finding: The plan's architectural justification asserts that `outbound_differ.py` 'already depends on rebar core' (used to justify placing identity.jira_account_id calls there), but a grep of the file confirms it has zero rebar core imports — no module-level or inline `from rebar.*` statements anywhere in the file. The sole rebar-core inline import in the differ cluster is in `outbound_fields.py`'s `_parent_clear_is_managed` helper (`from rebar.reducer._managed_refs`), not in `outbound_differ.py` itself. Adding `identity.jira_account_id` calls to `_assignee_resolver` (a closure inside `compute_outbound_mutations`) introduces a NEW rebar core dependency into `outbound_differ.py`, not an extension of a pre-existing one. If this import fails at test-isolation time (spec_from_file_location standalone load), the resolver will raise ImportError rather than degrade gracefully.
findings.location: Architectural constraint section / Section 2 (_assignee_resolver in outbound_differ.py)
findings.suggested_fix: In `_assignee_resolver`, import identity lazily: `try: from rebar._commands import identity as _identity; acct = _identity.jira_account_id(assignee) except ImportError: acct = None` — this degrades to the existing validate_assignee_exists path when rebar core is unavailable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in §3 (or §2) whether a `/user/search`-bootstrap-resolved accountId sets `is_account_id=True` or `False`, and why, resolving the conflict with §2's 'ONLY when from identity-mapping fast path' constraint.
findings.finding: The `is_account_id` flag assignment rule is ambiguous when the `/user/search` bootstrap (§3) successfully resolves an accountId: §2 defines `is_account_id=True` ONLY for the identity-mapping fast path, but §3 introduces a second accountId source whose `is_account_id` status is never stated, leaving implementors to choose whether bootstrap-resolved accountIds bypass `validate_assignee_exists`.
findings.location: Section 2 (`is_account_id` definition) cross-referenced against Section 3 (`/user/search` bootstrap path)

findings.finding: Section 2 states `is_account_id=True` ONLY when the accountId came from the identity-mapping fast path, but Section 3 describes a second accountId resolution path (/user/search bootstrap) without specifying whether it also sets `is_account_id=True`. If it does not, the bootstrapped accountId flows into `validate_assignee_exists`, which expects a display-name string, not an opaque accountId — potentially causing a spurious miss and degrading to unassigned even though a valid accountId was just resolved.
findings.location: Section 2 (`is_account_id` definition) vs Section 3 (/user/search bootstrap returning accountId)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section 3: 'When /user/search resolves an accountId, _assignee_resolver returns it with is_account_id=True (same flag semantics as the identity fast-path), so the accountId is submitted directly without passing through validate_assignee_exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full alert record schema for both new `kind` values (`outbound-reporter-unresolved` and `outbound-reporter-not-permitted`) including the required `key` (e.g. `f"{kind}:{jira_key}"`) dedup key, `timestamp_ns`, and `pass_id` fields, co-located with the §4 spec.
findings.finding: Two new alert `kind` strings (`outbound-reporter-unresolved`, `outbound-reporter-not-permitted`) are referenced in Section 4 but their full record dict schemas are never defined — specifically the required `key` (dedup key) and `timestamp_ns` and `pass_id` fields that every existing alert record in the codebase carries are absent from the plan's specification.
findings.location: Section 4 (Reporter by accountId) — alert_store.append(...) calls for both soft-fail paths; cross-referenced against src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_handlers.py:175–186 and run_differs.py:87–97
findings.suggested_fix: Add to §4: 'Record dict: `{"kind": "outbound-reporter-unresolved", "key": f"outbound-reporter-unresolved:{jira_key}", "jira_key": jira_key, "field": "reporter", "reason": ..., "pass_id": pass_id, "timestamp_ns": time.time_ns()}`; mirror for `outbound-reporter-not-permitted`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `GET /rest/api/3/user/search` is chosen over the already probe-validated `GET /rest/api/3/user/assignable/search` for the email→accountId bootstrap, and confirm the chosen endpoint's permission requirements are met in the target deployment (or switch to the existing endpoint if it satisfies the need).
findings.finding: The plan introduces `GET /rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` as a new endpoint for the bootstrap fallback, but the codebase already live-probe-validates `GET /rest/api/3/user/assignable/search?query=<email>&issueKey=<key>` which also returns `accountId` + `emailAddress` per field per user object. The prior-art endpoint may satisfy the same bootstrap need without requiring the 'Browse Users' global permission that `/user/search` demands. The plan does not explain why `user/assignable/search` (already exercised and permission-safe) cannot be used for the bootstrap, nor does it verify that `/user/search` is available in the deployment's permission model.
findings.location: Section 3 — `/user/search` bootstrap; `acli_rest.py` (new helper)
findings.suggested_fix: In `acli_rest.py`, implement the bootstrap helper against `/rest/api/3/user/assignable/search?query=<email>&project=<project_key>` (or `&issueKey=<key>` when one is available), reusing the same endpoint and exact-match logic already in `validate_assignee_exists`. This avoids the 'Browse Users' permission requirement and reuses probe-validated infrastructure. Only fall back to `/user/search` if `assignable/search` provably cannot be used for this case (e.g. no project scope available at call time).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `_assignee_cache` type annotation and all cache-store/cache-return sites in outbound_differ.py to the 3-tuple `tuple[str | None, bool, bool]` when `_assignee_resolver` return is extended.
findings.finding: The `_assignee_cache` dict in outbound_differ.py (line 471) is typed as `dict[str, tuple[str | None, bool]]` — a 2-tuple. When `_assignee_resolver` is changed to return a 3-tuple `(accountId|None, authoritative, is_account_id: bool)`, the cache type annotation becomes incorrect and any code reading from the cache will unpack a 3-tuple into a 2-tuple binding, causing ValueError.
findings.location: Section 2 (_assignee_resolver return extended to 3-tuple) / outbound_differ.py line 471 and line 486
findings.suggested_fix: Change `_assignee_cache: dict[str, tuple[str | None, bool]] = {}` to `dict[str, tuple[str | None, bool, bool]]` and ensure every `result = (...)` assignment that is cached is also a 3-tuple.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ensure `_assignee_cache` stores 3-tuples throughout: update every `_assignee_cache[assignee] = result` site so the non-identity path stores `(acct, authoritative, False)` and the identity fast-path stores `(acct, authoritative, True)`, eliminating 2-tuple/3-tuple mismatch on cache hits.
findings.finding: The `_assignee_cache` (typed as `dict[str, tuple[str | None, bool]]`) stores 2-tuples from the non-identity fallback path. When `_assignee_resolver`'s return is extended to a 3-tuple, cache hits from the existing `validate_assignee_exists` path would still return 2-tuples, causing `ValueError: not enough values to unpack` at every call site that unpacks the 3-tuple (including `outbound_fields.py` line 435). The plan acknowledges callers must be updated but does not address that cached values from the non-identity path must also be stored as 3-tuples.
findings.location: Section 2 — Assignee accountId fast-path; `outbound_differ.py` `_assignee_cache` at line 471
findings.suggested_fix: Change the cache type annotation to `dict[str, tuple[str | None, bool, bool]]` and update every assignment: e.g. the existing `result = (acct or None, True)` becomes `result = (acct or None, True, False)`; the identity fast-path result becomes `(account_id, True, True)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan that `dispatch_one.update_one` pops `_assignee_is_account_id` with a default of `False` (i.e., `fields.pop('_assignee_is_account_id', False)`) since the sentinel is only present in `changed` when the assignee fast-path was taken.
findings.finding: The plan is silent on how the `_assignee_is_account_id` sentinel interacts with the `_assignee_matches` convergence check that immediately precedes the `assignee_resolver` call in `_diff_fields`. When `_assignee_matches(local_val, jira_assignee)` returns True (already converged), the resolver is never called and no sentinel is set — meaning `changed` contains no `_assignee_is_account_id` key. `update_one`'s pop (`fields.pop('_assignee_is_account_id', False)`) would then default to `False` (correct). But the plan says `_diff_fields` 'writes the accountId into `changed['assignee']` and sets `changed['_assignee_is_account_id'] = is_account_id`' without clarifying this conditional nature, making the pop-with-default requirement implicit. The plan should state the sentinel is only present when assignee is in `changed` (i.e., the pop must use a default of `False`).
findings.location: Section 2 — Assignee accountId fast-path
findings.suggested_fix: Update Section 2 to read: '`dispatch_one.update_one` POPS `_assignee_is_account_id` off `fields` with default `False` (the sentinel is absent when assignee was already converged or no identity mapping was found)...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the canonical `sys.modules` key for the `alert_store` lazy-load inside `_update_one_apply_reporter` (must be `'rebar_reconciler.alert_store'` to match the existing test-patch target) and document the single `rebar.config.repo_root()` call used for both the `is_deduped` check and the `append` call.
findings.finding: The plan says `_update_one_apply_reporter` accesses `alert_store` 'by LAZY-LOAD (by file path, the same pattern `run_differs.py` / `fetcher.py` use)' and obtains `repo_root` via `rebar.config.repo_root()`, but `dispatch_one.py`'s `update_one` currently has NO `alert_store` lazy-load infrastructure, NO `rebar.config` import, and no `repo_root` acquisition in `update_one` (only `create_one` receives it as a parameter). The plan treats this as a pre-existing pattern in dispatch_one but it is wholly new, and the plan does not specify the canonical sys.modules key to use for the lazy-load (which matters for test patching — using the wrong key creates a dual-load that defeats test patches, as documented in fetcher.py line 122-128).
findings.location: Section 4 (_update_one_apply_reporter), 'LAZY-LOAD' claim

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a tie-breaking rule to the `jira_account_id` spec in §1 (e.g. 'ticket_id match takes priority over email match; if two identities both match by the same criterion, return None') to fully specify the decision protocol.
findings.finding: `jira_account_id`'s resolution protocol (§1) is unspecified for the case where a local assignee string simultaneously matches an identity's `ticket_id` AND a DIFFERENT identity's `email` — no tie-breaking rule or 'first match wins' ordering is given.
findings.location: Section 1 (`jira_account_id` function specification)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to §1: 'Matching priority: ticket_id match takes precedence over email match. If two distinct identities match by the same criterion, return None (ambiguous).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `outbound_fields.py` to `file_impact` with the note '(fix 2-tuple→3-tuple unpack of `_assignee_resolver` return at ~line 435)'.
findings.finding: outbound_fields.py is identified in §2 as a required unpack-site fix ('notably `outbound_fields.py` (~line 435, currently `acct, authoritative = assignee_resolver(...)`)') but is absent from file_impact, contradicting the body's own enumeration of changes.
findings.location: Section §2 ('All existing callers … notably outbound_fields.py') vs. file_impact section (omits outbound_fields.py)
findings.suggested_fix: In file_impact, change the `outbound_fields.py` entry to: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_fields.py` (fix `acct, authoritative = assignee_resolver(...)` unpack to 3-tuple at ~line 435; emit reporter into changed).

findings.finding: `outbound_fields.py` line 435 unpacks `_assignee_resolver`'s return as a 2-tuple (`acct, authoritative = assignee_resolver(...)`) but the plan extends `_assignee_resolver` to return a 3-tuple — this unpack will raise `ValueError: too many values to unpack` at runtime. Yet the `file_impact` entry for `outbound_fields.py` only mentions 'emit reporter into changed' and does not enumerate the 3-tuple unpack fix as a required edit.
findings.location: `file_impact` / `outbound_fields.py` / Section 2 (Assignee accountId fast-path)
findings.suggested_fix: In `file_impact` for `outbound_fields.py`, extend the description to: '(3-tuple unpack of `assignee_resolver` return + `_assignee_is_account_id` sentinel write + emit reporter into changed)'. In Section 2, explicitly call out that `_diff_fields` in `outbound_fields.py` is where the sentinel is actually written.

findings.finding: The _assignee_resolver 3-tuple extension breaks the call site in outbound_fields.py (_diff_fields line 435: 'acct, authoritative = assignee_resolver(local_val, jira_key)') but outbound_fields.py appears in file_impact only for 'emit reporter into changed' — the 3-tuple unpack update is not mentioned there, leaving the call-site update as an invisible mechanical change.
findings.location: Plan §2 (_assignee_resolver 3-tuple) and file_impact entry for outbound_fields.py
findings.suggested_fix: Change the file_impact line for outbound_fields.py to: '(emit reporter into changed; update _diff_fields 3-tuple unpack of assignee_resolver result: acct, authoritative, is_account_id = assignee_resolver(local_val, jira_key)).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in §3 how `_assignee_resolver` accesses the `/user/search` helper — either add a `client` parameter to `_assignee_resolver` (and update all call sites in file_impact), or state that `outbound_differ.py` imports `acli_rest` as a module and calls the function directly.
findings.finding: Section §3 says the differ 'calls the client's `/rest/api/3/user/search`' helper in acli_rest.py, but outbound_differ.py is a DIFFER (not a dispatcher) and currently has no client reference; the plan does not specify how `_assignee_resolver` obtains a client object — leaving implementers to choose between passing the client into `_assignee_resolver`, importing acli_rest as a module, or another approach.
findings.location: Section §3 ('the differ... best-effort calls the client's `/rest/api/3/user/search`... a helper in `acli_rest.py`') vs. Architectural constraint ('`acli.py` imports only within the rebar_reconciler package') and file_impact (outbound_differ.py entry)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Broaden the exception catch in `_update_one_apply_reporter` to also cover `urllib.error.URLError` and `socket.timeout` (or a common base like `OSError`), recording an alert of kind `outbound-reporter-not-permitted` (or a distinct `outbound-reporter-network-error`) and continuing — not just 4xx HTTPError.
findings.finding: The `set_reporter` error handling catches only `urllib.error.HTTPError` (4xx/5xx), but network-level failures (`urllib.error.URLError`, socket timeout, connection refused) are not caught. These would propagate up through `_update_one_apply_reporter` and could hard-fail `update_one` for any issue that has a reporter field, breaking the sync for that issue entirely rather than soft-degrading as intended.
findings.location: Section 4 (Reporter by accountId) — `_update_one_apply_reporter`; AC last bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `except urllib.error.HTTPError` with `except (urllib.error.URLError, OSError)` (HTTPError is a subclass of URLError, so this covers both), or use `except Exception` narrowed to the specific call site, to ensure all network failures degrade gracefully.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `_update_one_apply_reporter` wraps `rebar.config.repo_root()` in `Path(...)` before passing to `alert_store.append`, consistent with how other callers (e.g. `run_differs.py`, `fetcher.py`) handle the repo_root→Path conversion.
findings.finding: The plan routes `_update_one_apply_reporter` to obtain `alert_store` and `repo_root` via lazy-load and `rebar.config.repo_root()`, but `update_one`'s signature is explicitly stated as NOT changed. However, `alert_store.append(record, repo_root)` requires a `Path` for `repo_root`. If `rebar.config.repo_root()` returns a string (as it does in the existing codebase pattern), the `Path(...)` wrapping needs to happen inside `_update_one_apply_reporter`. The plan does not specify this conversion, and `alert_store.append` requires a `Path` (its signature is `def append(record: dict, repo_root: Path) -> None`). This is a minor but real mechanism gap.
findings.location: Section 4 — Reporter by accountId; `dispatch_one.py` `_update_one_apply_reporter`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `from pathlib import Path` and use `repo_root = Path(rebar.config.repo_root())` inside `_update_one_apply_reporter`.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `_update_one_apply_reporter` will use a LAZY-LOAD pattern 'the same pattern `run_differs.py` / `fetcher.py` use' to access `alert_store`, but `dispatch_one.py` currently contains NO lazy-load infrastructure — it imports via normal package-qualified imports (`from rebar_reconciler._errors import ...`, `from rebar_reconciler.binding_store import ...`). Adding lazy-load boilerplate to `dispatch_one.py` is non-trivial undescribed work; alternatively, since dispatch_one is a package module (not spec-loaded standalone in tests), a normal `from rebar_reconciler import alert_store` import would also work and is simpler, but neither path is specified.
findings.location: Section 4 (Reporter by accountId): '_update_one_apply_reporter accesses alert_store by LAZY-LOAD (by file path, the same pattern run_differs.py / fetcher.py use)'
findings.suggested_fix: Since `dispatch_one.py` is imported as a package module (not spec-loaded standalone), use a normal `from rebar_reconciler import alert_store as _alert_store_mod` deferred import inside `_update_one_apply_reporter`, or use `lazy_load('rebar_reconciler.alert_store', 'alert_store.py')` with the canonical key — but specify which and add the lazy_load import.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate ALL known call sites of `_assignee_resolver` by name (not 'any other unpack site') so the executor has a complete, exhaustive list to update.
findings.finding: The plan says 'All existing callers of `_assignee_resolver` must be updated to unpack the 3-tuple — notably `outbound_fields.py` (~line 435) and any other unpack site' but leaves discovering the complete set of callers to the executor ('any other unpack site' is an open-ended placeholder). If there are additional call sites in `dispatch_one.py` or test fixtures that also unpack the 2-tuple, the executor will find them only at runtime via ValueError.
findings.location: Section 2 (Assignee accountId fast-path), paragraph describing _assignee_resolver 3-tuple change
findings.suggested_fix: Add a grep/search command in the plan: `grep -rn 'assignee_resolver(' src/` and list every result by file+line. Name each site explicitly in the plan (e.g., `outbound_fields.py:435`, `dispatch_one.py:NNN`, any test fixtures) so the executor has a closed list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a simple within-run memo/cache (e.g., a dict keyed on email) to `_assignee_resolver`'s `/user/search` fallback so each unique email is queried at most once per reconciliation run.
findings.finding: The `/user/search` helper in `acli_rest.py` is called once per issue in `_assignee_resolver` (differ hot loop) with no within-run memoization; if multiple issues share the same unresolved assignee, the same email query is fired repeatedly.
findings.location: Section 3 (`/user/search` bootstrap); `outbound_differ.py` `_assignee_resolver`
findings.suggested_fix: In `_assignee_resolver` (or its caller `_diff_fields`), pass a mutable `_user_search_cache: dict[str, str | None]` defaultdict or use `functools.lru_cache` on the email→accountId helper so identical email lookups within a single run are short-circuited.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a log line (DEBUG or WARNING) when `jira_account_id` returns None for an assignee and when `/user/search` returns no exact match or ≥2 matches, so operators can observe mapping coverage and diagnose misconfiguration without needing to inspect Jira output.
findings.finding: Silent degradation on the assignee fast-path and `/user/search` bootstrap produces no observable signal. When `jira_account_id` returns None or `/user/search` finds no exact match, the plan says the flow degrades quietly to the existing string-match path with no log, metric, or alert — operators cannot distinguish 'mapping configured and working' from 'mapping missing for 80% of assignees' without inspecting raw Jira output.
findings.location: Section 2 (`_assignee_resolver` fast-path miss); Section 3 (`/user/search` miss/ambiguity path)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC5) asserting that when `jira_account_id(reporter)` returns None, `update_one` records an `outbound-reporter-unresolved` alert, drops the reporter field, applies other fields, and does not fail the sync.
findings.finding: The reporter-unresolved soft-fail path (when `jira_account_id(reporter)` returns None and an `outbound-reporter-unresolved` alert is recorded) has no AC requiring a test. AC5 covers only the HTTP 4xx → `outbound-reporter-not-permitted` case; the distinct `outbound-reporter-unresolved` alert kind, which fires on a missing mapping, is a new user-facing failure mode introduced in §4 with zero test coverage mandated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 / §4 'Reporter by accountId'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 with a second scenario: 'When the fixture identity has no jira mapping for the reporter, update_one records alert kind outbound-reporter-unresolved, other fields are still applied, and the sync does not fail.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before adding a new `/user/search` helper, verify whether `validate_assignee_exists(email, issue_key=jira_key)` (passing the identity's email as the query) already returns the correct accountId for the email-bootstrap path; if it does, reuse it instead of building a new REST helper.
findings.finding: The planned `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` helper in `acli_rest.py` rebuilds list-JSON-parsing + exact-emailAddress-match logic that is structurally identical to the existing `validate_assignee_exists` method, which already calls a Jira user search endpoint (`/rest/api/3/user/assignable/search`) and performs exact-match on `emailAddress` / `accountId` / `displayName`.
findings.location: Section 3 (`acli_rest.py` /user/search bootstrap helper)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `AcliClient.create_issue` (acli.py ~line 301) as a consumer of the assignee fast-path and decide whether it also needs an `assignee_is_account_id` bypass or a note explaining why CREATE is intentionally excluded.
findings.finding: AcliClient.create_issue (~acli.py:301) also calls validate_assignee_exists for the assignee fast-path and is not mentioned as a consumer requiring update. If a newly-created issue's local assignee has an identity mapping, the create path will still invoke the /user/assignable/search round-trip instead of using the accountId directly.
findings.location: Section 2 (Assignee accountId fast-path) / acli.py ~line 301 / file_impact
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `assignee_is_account_id: bool = False` handling to `create_issue` mirroring `update_issue`, or add an explicit in-scope note explaining CREATE is out of scope for this ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the RECON breadcrumb (or the `changed` key set passed to it) strips private sentinel keys (prefixed `_`) before logging, so `_assignee_is_account_id` does not appear in operational output.
findings.finding: The `_assignee_is_account_id` sentinel written into `changed` by `_diff_fields` will appear in the existing RECON breadcrumb log `f'changed=[{",".join(sorted(changed))}]'` in `_compute_outbound_update_mutation`. This leaks an internal implementation key into operational logs on every assignee fast-path hit, potentially confusing operators monitoring the breadcrumb output. The plan does not address this noise.
findings.location: Section 2 — Assignee accountId fast-path; `outbound_differ.py` breadcrumb at line 756
findings.suggested_fix: In the breadcrumb emit, filter out keys starting with `_`: `','.join(sorted(k for k in changed if not k.startswith('_')))`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a probe-validation note (or a live probe result) confirming that `GET /rest/api/3/user/search` returns a bare JSON list of user objects with `accountId` and `emailAddress` fields in the target Jira Cloud instance, and document the fallback behaviour when `emailAddress` is absent from results.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `GET /rest/api/3/user/search` returns a JSON list of user objects each with `accountId` + `emailAddress` fields (used for exact-email matching), but this capability is not probe-verified anywhere in the codebase — it is stated as a design assumption. The analogous claim for `/user/assignable/search` is backed by live-probe annotation in `validate_assignee_exists`. An unverified shape assumption here means a Jira instance returning a different response shape (e.g. a paginated object, or objects without `emailAddress` when email visibility is restricted) will silently degrade with no diagnostic.
findings.location: Section 3 — 'The v3 endpoint returns a JSON LIST of user objects each with accountId + emailAddress'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in Section 3 that Jira Cloud email-privacy settings can suppress `emailAddress` in `/user/search` responses, making exact-email bootstrap silently produce zero matches for affected users; add a note that operators should add explicit `mappings` entries for such users rather than relying on email bootstrap.
findings.finding: Jira Cloud's /rest/api/3/user/search endpoint respects per-user email privacy settings: when a user has enabled email visibility restrictions, their `emailAddress` field is absent or empty in the API response, causing the plan's exact-match strategy to produce zero results even when the user exists and has an accountId. The plan does not acknowledge this gap and presents the email-match as a reliable bootstrap path.
findings.location: Section 3 — /user/search bootstrap; AC line 'an identity with an email but no jira mapping resolves via a stubbed /user/search exact-email match'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in Section 3: 'When emailAddress is absent or empty in /user/search results (Jira Cloud email-privacy), the bootstrap degrades to a miss — indistinguishable from a non-existent user. Operators must add an explicit {provider:"jira", external_id: "<accountId>"} mapping for privacy-enabled users.' No code change required — the degradation path already handles it; the gap is in operator-visible diagnostics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (via a live probe or Jira docs reference) that PUT /rest/api/3/issue/{key} returns a 4xx (not a silent 204) when the caller lacks 'Modify Reporter' permission; if the response is silent, add a post-update GET to confirm the reporter field changed and record outbound-reporter-not-permitted if it did not.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a failed `set_reporter` call on Jira Cloud is detectable as HTTP 4xx (specifically the 'Modify Reporter not granted' scenario). However, Jira Cloud's behavior when a service account lacks the 'Modify Reporter' permission is not always a 4xx: in some Jira configurations the PUT /rest/api/3/issue/{key} call returns HTTP 200/204 but the reporter field is silently not updated. The plan's catch of `urllib.error.HTTPError` would not trigger in the silent-ignore case, leaving the reporter divergence undetected and no alert recorded.
findings.location: Section 4 — 'Failure is HTTP-ONLY (no subprocess): catch urllib.error.HTTPError (4xx = Modify-Reporter not granted)'

findings.finding: Section 4 describes a named, observable behavior — `alert_store.append(...)` of kind `outbound-reporter-unresolved` when `jira_account_id(reporter)` returns None — but no acceptance criterion covers it. AC5 tests only the HTTP 4xx path (`outbound-reporter-not-permitted`). The 'no mapping → skip + alert' path is a described deliverable with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Section 4 (`_update_one_apply_reporter` soft-fail on unresolved reporter) vs AC5

findings.finding: The `outbound-reporter-unresolved` soft-fail path — triggered when `jira_account_id(reporter)` returns None — is described in Section 4 ('when `jira_account_id(reporter)` returns None ... soft-fail `alert_store.append(...)` of kind `outbound-reporter-unresolved`') but is absent from all five ACs. AC5 only covers the HTTP 4xx path (`outbound-reporter-not-permitted`), leaving this failure path untested by any acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Section 4 (Reporter by accountId); Acceptance Criteria AC5

findings.finding: `dispatch_one.py` currently has zero imports from `rebar` core (only `rebar_reconciler.*` imports), but the plan's `_update_one_apply_reporter` calls `rebar.config.repo_root()` — introducing a new `rebar` core import into a module that has never had one. This is not acknowledged in the plan and may conflict with the module's existing import contract (or require an explicit 'flow-layer exception' note).
findings.location: Section 4 (Reporter by accountId) / `file_impact` entry for `dispatch_one.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one acceptance criterion (or explicitly defer to a named child ticket with rationale) that exercises the `/user/search` accountId extraction and `set_reporter` PUT against a real or contract-test Jira endpoint, not a stub that bypasses the new behavior.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the new live-Jira REST paths (`/rest/api/3/user/search` and `/rest/api/3/issue/{key}` PUT for reporter) end-to-end against a real Jira instance. The plan explicitly states 'Tests use a fixture identity + a stubbed client (no live Jira)' with no named child ticket or rationale deferring end-to-end coverage. Both acli_rest.py helpers are new external-boundary code paths that will never execute during CI validation.
findings.location: Scope section ('Tests use a fixture identity + a stubbed client (no live Jira)'); AC4 and AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add an optional integration-test AC guarded by a `JIRA_TEST_INSTANCE` env var that calls a real sandbox Jira and asserts the returned accountId shape; or (b) add a contract/schema assertion AC that validates the acli_rest.py parsers against a recorded real Jira response fixture (not a hand-rolled stub), and name it explicitly so it can't be satisfied by the existing mocks.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete `pytest` command (file path and/or `-k` pattern) to each AC so an agent can produce targeted evidence of that criterion's satisfaction.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion in the plan includes a concrete proving command (e.g., a `pytest` invocation with a `-k` pattern or file path) — all five ACs end with '(test)', '(test both)', or '(test all three)' without specifying how an agent would run them to produce evidence. Per E6(a), every completion-relevant claim needs a concrete proving command that produces evidence on success.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all five ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to each AC a command such as: `pytest src/rebar/_commands/test_identity.py -k 'resolve_mapping or jira_account_id or identity_email'` (AC1), `pytest ... -k test_assignee_fast_path` (AC2), etc. Use the planned test file names from file_impact or add a `test_files` entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing note to §2: either (a) all 3-tuple unpack sites are updated in the same atomic commit as the `_assignee_resolver` change, or (b) use an expand-contract step (return `(acct, auth, False)` as a named-tuple/dataclass so old `a, b = ...` callers still fail-fast but the change is isolated); document the rollback as 'revert the single atomic commit'.
findings.finding: The `_assignee_resolver` return-type change from a 2-tuple to a 3-tuple is a breaking interface change that the plan acknowledges ('else ValueError: too many values to unpack') but provides no expand-contract sequencing or rollback/back-out path.
findings.location: Section §2 ('_assignee_resolver's return is EXTENDED from … to a 3-tuple … All existing callers … must be updated')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to §2: 'All changes to `_assignee_resolver` and every call-site unpack MUST land in a single atomic commit so no intermediate state is deployable. Rollback = revert that commit entirely. Expand-contract is not required since this is an internal function with a closed set of callers enumerated above.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the bool-flag sentinel pattern, the `_direct_rest_put_raw` vs `_direct_rest_put` distinction, and the `acli.py` core-library boundary constraint in CLAUDE.md or an ADR so future contributors don't silently violate the layering.
findings.finding: The novel architectural pattern — bool-flag sentinel threading (`_assignee_is_account_id`) from the differ through dispatch_one into acli to avoid a circular import, plus the `_direct_rest_put_raw` vs `_direct_rest_put` distinction — constitutes a new cross-component contract and pipeline stage but no ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc update is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Architectural constraint section; Section 2 (`_assignee_is_account_id` sentinel); Section 4 (`_direct_rest_put_raw`); `file_impact`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Outbound Jira Adapter Architecture` section to CLAUDE.md (or a new `docs/adr/NNN-jira-outbound-identity.md`) covering: (1) why `acli.py` receives only bool+resolved-string (never imports rebar core), (2) the `_direct_rest_put_raw` vs `_direct_rest_put` behavioral difference, and (3) the `_assignee_is_account_id` sentinel-in-dict pattern and why it is not a dataclass field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a short timeout (e.g., 5 s) on the `/user/search` HTTP call in `acli_rest.py`, and catch `urllib.error.URLError` / `TimeoutError` in the fallback so a hung Jira API degrades rather than blocks.
findings.finding: The `/user/search` HTTP call is synchronous and in the per-issue differ hot path, but no timeout is specified for this network call; a slow or hung Jira API response would block the entire reconciliation loop indefinitely.
findings.location: Section 3 (`/user/search` bootstrap); `acli_rest.py` helper
findings.suggested_fix: In the `/user/search` helper, pass `timeout=5` to `urllib.request.urlopen` (or the equivalent in the existing REST helper pattern) and wrap the call in `except (urllib.error.URLError, TimeoutError): return None` so any network-level failure degrades cleanly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a short, configurable timeout (e.g., 5–10 s) for both the `/user/search` and `set_reporter` HTTP calls in `acli_rest.py`, and handle the resulting `socket.timeout`/`urllib.error.URLError` as graceful misses (degrade, do not propagate).
findings.finding: Neither the `/user/search` HTTP call nor the `set_reporter` REST call specifies a request timeout. If Jira is slow or hung, both calls block indefinitely, stalling the reconciler for every issue processed in that run — a single slow Jira response degrades the entire sync job, not just the affected issue.
findings.location: Section 3 (`/user/search` bootstrap in `acli_rest.py`); Section 4 (`set_reporter` via `_direct_rest_put_raw`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live/end-to-end acceptance criterion (or cite a named child ticket) that exercises the `/user/search` exact-email-match logic and `set_reporter` REST call against a real or recorded-HTTP test harness, ensuring the stub-only suite does not constitute the sole acceptance gate for these newly-defaulted REST paths.
findings.finding: Both new REST-boundary paths — the `/user/search` email→accountId helper in `acli_rest.py` and `set_reporter` via `_direct_rest_put_raw` — are validated SOLELY through a stubbed client ('no live Jira'), so the JSON parsing, URL construction, exact-email-match logic, and raw-PUT payload shape are never exercised against the real boundary. No named child ticket or integration-test gate is cited for live exercise of these newly-defaulted paths.
findings.location: Scope section ('Tests use a fixture identity + a stubbed client (no live Jira)') / §3 /user/search bootstrap / §4 acli_rest.set_reporter
findings.suggested_fix: Name a child ticket for integration/contract testing of acli_rest.py REST helpers, or add an AC that runs against a recorded HTTP fixture (e.g., responses library or VCR) that exercises the real urllib + JSON parsing code paths in acli_rest.py rather than bypassing them at the client-object level.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `resolve_mapping` has ≥2 concrete in-scope consumers in THIS ticket; if `jira_account_id` is the sole in-scope caller, inline the scan loop into `jira_account_id` and expose `resolve_mapping` only when 2f13 is in scope.
findings.finding: `resolve_mapping` is introduced as a public seam in `identity.py` with only one in-scope consumer (`jira_account_id` internally), making it a premature abstraction that does not satisfy the Rule-of-Three at delivery time.
findings.location: Section 1 (`src/rebar/_commands/identity.py`) / Scope section ('Out: inbound ghost minting (2f13)')
findings.suggested_fix: Inline the mapping scan into `jira_account_id`. Add `resolve_mapping` as a separate PR when 2f13 is scoped, at which point you will have two real callers and can verify the interface fits both.

findings.finding: The `_assignee_resolver` 3-tuple consumer is identified as `_diff_fields` in `outbound_differ.py`, but `_diff_fields` actually lives in `outbound_fields.py` (call site at line 435 unpacks a 2-tuple). The plan's decision rule for writing `changed['_assignee_is_account_id']` is attributed to the wrong module.
findings.location: Section 2 (Assignee accountId fast-path) / outbound_differ.py vs outbound_fields.py
findings.suggested_fix: In file_impact, add `outbound_fields.py` with note: '(_diff_fields: unpack 3-tuple from assignee_resolver, write _assignee_is_account_id sentinel into changed dict)'.

findings.finding: `identity_email` (introduced in Section 1 as a required seam function) has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion. The AC1 only names `resolve_mapping` and `jira_account_id`, leaving `identity_email` untested by any stated AC.
findings.location: Section 1 (`identity_email`); Acceptance Criteria AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: 'and `identity_email` returns the matched identity's `email` for an id-match and an email-match (case-insensitive), and None when absent.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
